Arguments.prompt: Query the USGS Earthquake API at https://earthquake.usgs.gov/fdsnws/event/1/query with the following parameters: format=geojson, minimum magnitude of 2.0, and a bounding box covering the western United States defined by minimum latitude 32, maximum latitude 49, minimum longitude -125, and maximum longitude -114. Set the time window to cover the past 30 days from today's date (compute the start date as today minus 30 days and the end date as today). Parse the returned GeoJSON response and extract individual earthquake feature records. For each feature, capture the following fields: magnitude (from properties.mag), depth in kilometers (from geometry.coordinates index 2), latitude (from geometry.coordinates index 1), and longitude (from geometry.coordinates index 0). Also record the query start date and end date as metadata. Assemble all extracted records into a tabular dataset with columns: magnitude, depth_km, latitude, longitude. Drop any rows where magnitude or depth_km is null or missing. Save the cleaned earthquake dataset as a CSV file named earthquake_data.csv. Also save a small JSON metadata file named earthquake_meta.json containing the query start date, query end date, and the total count of cleaned earthquake records.

Download the US Census Bureau state boundary cartographic boundary shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip. Unzip the archive and load the shapefile using geopandas. Filter the GeoDataFrame to retain only the following eight states by their NAME field: California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Idaho, Arizona, Utah, and Montana. Save the filtered GeoDataFrame as a GeoJSON file named western_states.geojson. This file will be used by the next step to render state boundary outlines on the earthquake map.

Using the earthquake dataset CSV, the earthquake metadata JSON, and the western states GeoJSON file produced by the previous steps — all discoverable via the data registry — construct a publication-quality earthquake map visualization as follows.

Load the earthquake dataset CSV containing columns: magnitude, depth_km, latitude, longitude. Load the metadata JSON to retrieve the query start date, query end date, and total event count. Load the western states GeoJSON using geopandas.

Compute visual properties for each earthquake marker:
- Marker size: use an exponential scale where size = 80 * (2 ** (magnitude - mag_min)), where mag_min is the minimum magnitude value present in the cleaned dataset.
- Marker color: map each earthquake's depth_km value to the 'plasma' colormap using matplotlib's automatic scaling based on the dataset's actual minimum and maximum depth_km values.

Create a matplotlib figure with a size of 12 by 8 inches. Set both the figure background color and the axes background color to the hex color #0d1b2a (dark navy).

Plot the state boundary polygons from the western states GeoDataFrame. Draw only the boundary outlines (no fill) in the hex color #cccccc (light gray) with a line width of 0.8.

Overlay a dashed grid on the axes using linestyle '--', alpha transparency of 0.3, and line width of 0.5, colored in light gray.

Plot the earthquake events as a scatter plot using the computed exponential marker sizes and the 'plasma' colormap with depth_km-based coloring. Apply black edge colors to each marker with a line width of 0.3.

After plotting, set the axes aspect ratio to 'equal'. Clamp the x-axis limits to -125 to -114 and the y-axis limits to 32 to 49.

Add a colorbar to the figure labeled 'Depth (km)' reflecting the depth-to-color mapping.

Add a legend positioned outside the map to the right, using loc='upper left' and bbox_to_anchor=(1.15, 1.0). The legend should display five representative magnitude entries for M2, M3, M4, M5, and M6, using the same exponential size formula (with mag_min from the dataset) to compute the representative sizes. Style the legend with markerscale=0.4, labelspacing=1.2, and handletextpad=1.0. Set the legend background color to #0d1b2a and legend text color to white.

Set the figure title as a single line formatted as: 'Western US Earthquakes (M2.0+) | <start_date> to <end_date> | <N> events', where start_date and end_date are the actual query window dates from the metadata JSON and N is the total cleaned event count from the metadata JSON. Style the title text in white.

Style all axis tick labels and axis labels in white for readability against the dark background.

Save the completed figure as a PNG image file named earthquake_map.png at high resolution (dpi=150). This file is the final deliverable and will be automatically uploaded to S3 by the system.
